content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

December 22, 1976

76-12-129

Honorable Wade N. Shifflett
Judge of the Municipal Court
1150 Third Street
Napa, California 94558

Dear Judge Shifflett:

At the outset, let me apologize for the long delay in
responding to your letter of May 26, 1976. Apparently the letter
was mislaced and our error was discovered only after receipt of
your second letter. With respect to the second letter, we pur-
posely delayed our response pending adoption of 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18723, which establishes closing dates and filing dates
for anniversary Statements of Economic Interests, so that we
could definitively respond to certain of your questions. In any
event, the answers to your inquiries are as follows.

1. Your first Statement of Economic Interests is due no
later than March 1, 1977.

2. The Statement must include income received and invest-
ments and interests in real property held at any time during the
period from May 5, 1976, through December 31, 1976. (Gov. Code
Section 87203 and 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18723.)

3. Assuming you remain a municipal court judge and seek re-
election in 1978, you must file Statements as follows:

A. An annual statement no later than March 1, 1978, dis-
closing income received and investments and interests
in real property held at any time during the period
from January 1, 1977, through December 31, 1977 (Gov.
Code Section 87203 and 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18723);

B. A statement at the time you file your declaration
of candidacy for the 1978 election setting forth your
investments and interests in real property as of the
filing date (Gov. Code Section 87201);

C. If you are defeated, a statement within 30 days after
you leave office disclosing income received and in-
vestments and interests in real property held at any

6

Honorable Wade N. Shifflett
Page 2
December 22, 1976

time during the period from January 1, 1978, to the
date on which you leave office (Gov. Code Section
87204);

D. If you are reelected an annual statement on March 1,
1979, disclosing income received and investments and
interests in real property held at any time during
the period from January 1, 1978, through December 31,
1978, (Gov. Code Section 87203 and 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18723).

Obviously, an amendment to the Political Reform Act or Commission
regulations could change these filing dates. However, no changes
are presently being contemplated by the Commission.

4. With respect to rental income, you must disclose the
gross amount received. However, you need not disclose the name
and address of any tenant unless your pro rata share of the gross
rental payments from that tenant equals $10,000 or more during
the calendar year. Gov. Code Section 87207(b)(3). Finally, on
your first Statement (covering the period from May 5, 1976, to
December 31, 1976), rental income from property outside the state
must be disclosed in the same manner as rental income from inside
the state.

The following additional observations should be made
concerning rental income. New legislation, effective January 1,
1977, provides that future Statements need only contain income
earned in the state or from a business entity which is doing, has
done, or intends to do business within the state. Moreover, you
should be aware that with respect to any income which must be reported, the source of that income (in this case the rental property) must be identified. Lastly, the value of the out-of-state
property need not be disclosed as an investment or an interest in
real property, either this year or in the future.

5. In reporting the value of an interest in real property,
you should report the fair market value of your equity interest
in the property (that is, the fair market value of the property
minus your mortgage). In order to comply with the reporting obligations imposed by the Political Reform Act, it is only necessary
that you estimate whether your equity interest is less than
$10,000 in value, less than $100,000 in value, or more than
$100,000 in value. Since reporting is based on the fair market
value of your equity interest, the value which the assessor places
on the property for tax purposes has little relevance to your disclosure obligation and cannot be relied on.

6. Generally, property disclosed on your Statement should be
identified by its street address. If the property does not have a

Honorable Wade N. Dufflett
Page 3
December 22, 1976

street address, any other reasonable means of identifying the lo-
cation of the property may be used, including its parcel number.

7. All stocks and bonds issued by a business entity which
owns an interest in real property in the state, does or plans to
do business in the state, or has done business in the state dur-
ing the two years prior to the closing date of your Statement
must be disclosed as investments (Schedule 721A). Gov. Code
Section 82034. However, dividends, interest, or any other re-
turn on a security registered with the Securities and Exchange
Commission need not be disclosed as income (Schedule 721D). Gov. Code Section 82030(b)(8). In addition, capital gains real-
ized on the sale of capital assets need not be disclosed as in-
come if through the exercise of reasonable diligence you do not
know or have reason to know the identity of the purchaser.
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18729.

8. Gov. Code Section 82030(b)(6), excludes devises or inheri-
tances from the definition of income. Accordingly, it would not
be necessary to disclose the receipt of an interest in a testa-
mentary trust as income on Schedule 721D. However, if you re-
ceive payments of $250 or more a year from the trust, you must
report such payments as income and identify the trust as the
source of that income.

In addition, investments of $1,000 or more owned by the
testamentary trust to which you refer must be disclosed on your
Statement of Economic Interests. Gov. Code Section 82034 defines
"investment" to include any investment of $1,000 or more owned by
a trust in which the filer's pro rata share is ten percent or
greater. Your letter stipulates that your mother is an income
beneficiary of the trust and that you are the sole remainderman.
Although the determination of the amount of your pro rata share
of the trust under these circumstances poses a unique question of
statutory interpretation, we have concluded that the purposes of
the Political Reform Act are best served by deeming your interest
in the trust, for purposes of disclosure, to be 100%. You will be
the sole beneficiary under the trust when the present life interest
expenses and there is nothing that suggests the corpus of the trust
will be depleted before it passes to you. Indeed, as trustee you
may be able to prevent this. Accordingly, you should report on
your Statement of Economic Interests any investment owned by the
trust of $1,000 or more. I wish to emphasize, however, that the
term "investment" includes only a financial interest in or a secu-
rity issued by a business entity which owns an interest in real
property in the state, does or plans to do business in the state,
or has done business in the state within two years prior to the
closing date of your Statement.

Honorable Wade N. [illegible]
Page 4
December 22, 1976

     If you are dissatisfied with the advice contained in
this letter and wish to seek a formal opinion of the Commission
on this issue, you may do so. Again, I apologize for the long
delay in responding to your letter. If you have any further ques-
tions, please contact me.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Alfredo Flores
                                        Research Specialist
                                        Conflict of Interest Division

AF:jo

NAPA COUNTY                                           NAPA JUDICIAL DISTRICT

WADE N. SHIFFLETT, JR.

JUDGE                                    1150 THIRD STREET · P.O. BOX 209 · NAPA, CALIFORNIA 94558
                                         AREA CODE 707/226-7464

                                         September 15, 1976


State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Gentlemen:

     Enclosed is a copy of a letter written to you on May 26,
1976 by Judge Shifflett. He has had no reply and is unable
to proceed without answers to his questions.

     Thank you for your cooperation.

                                         Yours very truly,


                                         _____________________
                                         (Mrs.) Maxine Klein, Clerk


mk

May 26, 1976


State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 607
Sacramento, California 95804

Gentlemen:

     In response to your memo of May 26th, I imagine you will
receive many inquiries from many confused judges so as such
I should like to get my oar in early.

     I was elected a justice court judge 1970 term January 2,
1971 - December 31, 1976. On March 2, 1976 by virtue of an act
of the legislature and ordinance of the board of supervisors
consolidating my district with another, my court was created
as a municipal court making us a court of record. The Government
Code provides that I succeed to Judge of the Municipal Court
and since it also provides that no election can be held for the
position within ten months of such suspension, my term as a
Municipal Court Judge runs until December 31, 1976 and requires
me to stand for reelection in June 1978 rather than June 1976
as would have been the case had I not been organized as a
Municipal Court. Now:

     (1) When do I file first statement?
     (2) What period of time does that report cover?
     (3) After that, when are subsequent statements
         required and what period of time do they cover?

     In reporting rental income over $250.00, is that gross or net?
Must you disclose the name of the tenant? What about rental property
in another state?

     In reporting interests in real property, can you use the
county assessor's appraisal of fair market value? Can you use
the assessor's parcel number to describe property?

-2-

What stocks or bonds must be included? You say stock on doesn't have to be. What about bonds on NYSF? Is the classification the same for set 721-A as for 721-D?

In 1946 my father died and left property, both real and personal,in trust for my mother with a small income provision for me when the income exceeds a certain amount and with me as the first remainderman contingent on surviving my mother. I am a trustee serving with Wells Fargo Bank until recently and now having along. Your annual appeal of trustee but does not define that as including testamentary trusts. However, I notice 721-D excludes property received by inheritance. Where does this leave me with regard to (1) capital (2) income of the trust?

I am sorry to impose on you but I am simply bewildered.

Respectfully,

WADE N. SHAPPLETT, JR.

WING rndc

NAPA COUNTY
NAPA JUDICIAL DISTRICT

WADE N. SHIFFLETT, JR.

1150 THIRD STREET · P.O. BOX 209 · NAPA, CALIFORNIA 94558

JUDGE
AREA CODE 707/226-7464

May 26, 1976

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Gentlemen:

In response to your memo of May 26th, I imagine you will
receive many inquiries from many confused judges so as such
I should like to get my oar in early.

I was elected a justice court judge 1970 term January 1,
1971 - December 31, 1976. On March 1, 1976 by virtue of an act
of the legislature and ordinance of the board of supervisors
consolidating my district with another, my court was organized
as a municipal court making me a court of record. The Government
Code provides that I succeed to Judge of the Municipal Court
and since it also provides that no election can be held for this
position within ten months of such succession, my term as a
Municipal Court Judge runs until December 31, 1978 and requires
me to stand for reelection in June 1978 rather than June 1976
as would have been the case had I not been organized as a
Municipal Court. Now:

(1) When do I file first statement?
(2) What period of time does that report cover?
(3) After that, when are subsequent statements
required and what period of time do they cover?

In reporting rental income over $250.00, is that gross or net?
Must you disclose the name of the tenant? What about rental property
in another state?

In reporting interests in real property, can you use the
county assessor's appraisal of fair market value? Can you use
the assessor's parcel number to describe property?

-2-

What stocks or bonds must be included? You say stock on
NYSE doesn't have to be. What about bonds on NYSE? Is the
classification the same for set 721-A as for 721-D?

In 1946 my father died and left property, both real and
personal,in trust for my mother with a small income provision
for me when the income exceeds a certain amount and win me as
the first remainderman contingent on surviving my mother. I am
a trustee serving with Wells Fargo Bank until recently and now
serving alone. Your manual speaks of trusts but does notdefine
that as including testamentary trusts. However, I notice 721-D
excludes property received by inheritance. Where does this
leave me with regard to (1) capital (2) income of the trust?

I am sorry to impose on you but I am simply bewildered.

Respectfully,

[signature]
WADE N. SHIFFLETT, JR.


WNS:mk

sections.question_synthetic: A municipal court judge seeks clarification on Statement of Economic Interests filing requirements, including deadlines, disclosure of rental income from out-of-state property, valuation of real property interests, reporting of stocks and bonds, and treatment of testamentary trust interests.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advises that the judge's first Statement is due March 1, 1977, covering May 5-December 31, 1976; rental income must be disclosed at gross amount with tenant identity disclosed only if pro rata share exceeds $10,000; real property equity interests should be valued at fair market value minus mortgage; stocks and bonds in business entities must be disclosed as investments; and the judge should report testamentary trust investments of $1,000+ when the life interest terminates and the corpus becomes distributable.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both FPPC response letter (main body) and follow-up correspondence from the judge with clarifying questions. No formal 'QUESTION' or 'CONCLUSION' section headers present; structured as numbered advisory points. Some OCR degradation affects readability of portions of the text. Judge's follow-up questions at end suggest continued uncertainty about stocks/bonds disclosure requirements.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: A municipal court judge seeks clarification on Statement of Economic Interests filing requirements, including deadlines, disclosure of rental income from out-of-state property, valuation of real property interests, reporting of stocks and bonds, and treatment of testamentary trust interests.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC advises that the judge's first Statement is due March 1, 1977, covering May 5-December 31, 1976; rental income must be disclosed at gross amount with tenant identity disclosed only if pro rata share exceeds $10,000; real property equity interests should be valued at fair market value minus mortgage; stocks and bonds in business entities must be disclosed as investments; and the judge should report testamentary trust investments of $1,000+ when the life interest terminates and the corpus becomes distributable.

embedding.summary: FPPC provides detailed guidance to a municipal court judge regarding Statement of Economic Interests filing deadlines, income disclosure requirements for rental properties (including out-of-state properties), property valuation methodology, investment reporting requirements, and treatment of interests in testamentary trusts.
